coredata.dc:title: Electrostatically-driven self-assembly of copper nanoclusters and carbon dots for quantitative protamine and heparin determination
coredata.dc:creator: Alkahtani, Saad A.
Mahmoud, Ashraf M.
Mohamed, Rasha M.K.
El-Wekil, Mohamed M.
coredata.dc:description: A novel nanosystem composed of red-emissive thiolate-capped copper nanoclusters (GSH-CuNCs) and blue-emissive polyethyleneimine-tailored carbon dots (B-CDs+) was utilized for the quantitative determination of protamine and heparin. Addition of B-CDs+ to a solution containing GSH-CuNCs resulted in a significant increase in the fluorescence emission of GSH-CuNCs due to their self-assembly through an electrostatic interaction. However, in the presence of protamine, a polycationic protein, the fluorescence emission of GSH-CuNCs at 590 nm was decreased as a result of adsorbing protamine on GSH-CuNCs surface instead of B-CDs+. This led to disassembling of GSH-CuNCs, while the fluorescence emission of B-CDs+ at 470 nm remained unchanged during the determination. In the presence of heparin, a negatively charged molecule, the fluorescence emission of GSH-CuNCs nanosystem was recovered due to strong electrostatic interaction between protamine and heparin. The displaced B-CDs+ aggregated the GSH-CuNCs, restoring its fluorescence emission. Under optimized conditions, the fluorescence responses were increased with the protamine and heparin concentrations in the range of 0.004–40 µg/mL and 0.01–28 µg/mL, with LODs (S/N = 3) of 0.001 µg/mL and 0.004 µg/mL, respectively. The nanosystem offers several merits, including an ultra-low LODs, wide dynamic linear range, good anti-interference, satisfactory detection reliability, and short response time. Importantly, this strategy was successfully devoted to estimate protamine and heparin in urine and serum samples, and the results were found to be acceptable.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Protamine
Heparin
Cationic carbon dots
GSH-CuNCs
Self-assembly
Electrostatic interaction

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of blue-emitting cationic carbon dots (B-CDs+)
The AIEE of GSH-CuNCs by B-CDs+
Ratiometric perception of protamine and heparin and variables optimization
Ratiometric quantitative estimation of protamine and heparin

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are thankful to the Deanship of Scientific Research at Najran University for funding this work under the National Research Priorities funding program grant code (NU/DRP/MRC/12/22).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image (A), UV/Vis and fluorescence emission spectra (B), XRD (C), FT-IR (D), and excitation-dependent emission (E) of B-CDs+.
TEM image (A), UV/Vis and fluorescence emission spectra (B), EDX (C), FT-IR (D), and excitation-dependent emission (E) of GSH-CuNCs.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of the B-CDs+@GSH-CuNCs nanosystem recorded following the sequential addition of protamine (0–40 μg/mL). (B) Calibration plot between F470/F590 and concentration of protamine. (C) Fluorescence emission spectra of B-CDs+@GSH-CuNCs/protamine nanosystem after addition of different concentrations of heparin (0–28 μg/mL). (D) Calibration plot between F590/F470 and concentration of heparin. Concentration of protamine used in (C) and (D) is 40 µg/mL.

(A) The selectivity of B-CDs+@GSH-CuNCs nanosystem towards protamine detection (15 µg/mL) in the presence of 150 µg/mL interfering species. (B) The selectivity of B-CDs+@GSH-CuNCs/protamine nanosystem towards heparin detection (15 µg/mL) in the presence of 150 µg/mL interfering species.
Steps for manufacturing of B-CDs+@ GSH-CuNCs system and its utility for the determination of protamine and heparin.
Applications of B-CDs+@GSH-CuNCs nanosystem for protamine detection in serum and urine samples (n = 5).
Applications of B-CDs+@GSH-CuNCs/protamine system for heparin detection in serum and urine samples (n = 5).

head.ce:title: Electrostatically-driven self-assembly of copper nanoclusters and carbon dots for quantitative protamine and heparin determination

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Clinical Pharmacy, College of Pharmacy, Najran University, Najran, Saudi Arabia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Clinical Pharmacy, College of Pharmacy, Najran University, Najran, Saudi Arabia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Chemistry, College of Pharmacy, Najran University, Najran, Saudi Arabia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutical Chemistry, College of Pharmacy, Najran University, Najran, Saudi Arabia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, Jouf University, P.O. Box 2014, Sakaka, Saudi Arabia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, Jouf University, P.O. Box 2014, Sakaka, Saudi Arabia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Assiut University, Assiut, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Assiut University, Assiut, Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel nanosystem composed of red-emissive thiolate-capped copper nanoclusters (GSH-CuNCs) and blue-emissive polyethyleneimine-tailored carbon dots (B-CDs+) was utilized for the quantitative determination of protamine and heparin. Addition of B-CDs+ to a solution containing GSH-CuNCs resulted in a significant increase in the fluorescence emission of GSH-CuNCs due to their self-assembly through an electrostatic interaction. However, in the presence of protamine, a polycationic protein, the fluorescence emission of GSH-CuNCs at 590 nm was decreased as a result of adsorbing protamine on GSH-CuNCs surface instead of B-CDs+. This led to disassembling of GSH-CuNCs, while the fluorescence emission of B-CDs+ at 470 nm remained unchanged during the determination. In the presence of heparin, a negatively charged molecule, the fluorescence emission of GSH-CuNCs nanosystem was recovered due to strong electrostatic interaction between protamine and heparin. The displaced B-CDs+ aggregated the GSH-CuNCs, restoring its fluorescence emission. Under optimized conditions, the fluorescence responses were increased with the protamine and heparin concentrations in the range of 0.004–40 µg/mL and 0.01–28 µg/mL, with LODs (S/N = 3) of 0.001 µg/mL and 0.004 µg/mL, respectively. The nanosystem offers several merits, including an ultra-low LODs, wide dynamic linear range, good anti-interference, satisfactory detection reliability, and short response time. Importantly, this strategy was successfully devoted to estimate protamine and heparin in urine and serum samples, and the results were found to be acceptable.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Heparin, a widely used anticoagulant, is commonly used to prevent and treat blood clots. It is a glycosaminoglycan, which is a type of long, linear carbohydrate molecule that is found in many tissues in the body, particularly in connective tissues and blood vessels [1]. Heparin is used for a wide range of medical conditions, including pulmonary embolism, deep vein thrombosis, acute coronary syndrome, and atrial fibrillation. It is also used during surgeries, such as heart surgeries and dialysis procedures, to prevent blood clots from forming [2]. Although heparin is generally considered safe and effective, it can cause side effects, such as bleeding, osteoporosis, and heparin-induced thrombocytopenia with long-term use. Therefore, careful monitoring of heparin dosage and patient's response is necessary [3]. Protamine (isoelectric point = 13.8) is a small, arginine-rich protein that is commonly found in the sperm of fish and other animals. It has been used as a medication to reverse the anticoagulant effects of heparin, a widely used anticoagulant drug. Protamine is generally considered safe and effective, but it can cause side effects, such as allergic reactions, hypotension, and bradycardia. Therefore, careful monitoring of protamine dosage and patient's response is necessary [5]. To date, heparin and protamine were determined using HPLC [6,7], capillary electrophoresis [8,9], electrochemical [10], and colorimetry [11,12]. Among them, fluorometric methods have gained more attentions as a result of simple operation, high sensitivity, and rapidity [13–16]. Copper nanoclusters (CuNCs) are a class of nanomaterials composed of a few to several hundred copper atoms. They have unique optical, electronic, and catalytic properties, and have attracted significant interest in various fields, including electronics, photonics, biomedicine, and catalysis [17]. They have been found to exhibit strong luminescence, which is tunable by varying their size and surface chemistry. They have also been used as catalysts in various reactions, such as oxidation, reduction, and coupling reactions. In addition, CuNCs have shown promise in biomedical applications, such as bioimaging, drug delivery, and photothermal therapy [18]. CuNCs have been widely used as sensing platforms for the detection of various analytes. Due to their unique optical and electronic properties, they can be used as fluorescent probes or electrochemical sensors for the detection of targets such as metal ions, small molecules, and biomolecules [19]. They can be synthesized with different surface functionalizations to improve their selectivity and sensitivity towards specific analytes. For example, thiol-capped CuNCs have been used for the detection of heavy metal ions such as Hg2+, Pb2+, and Cu2+ due to the strong affinity of thiol groups towards these metal ions [20]. In addition, CuNCs can be rationally mixed with blue fluorescent nanoparticles e.g. carbon dots (CDs) for ratiometric determination of (bio) molecules. For example, He et al. synthesized blue emissive carbon dots (b-CDs) and red emissive CuNCs for ratiometric determination of dopamine (DA), using aminophenylboronic acid as a receptor for DA [21]. Another example, Bu et al. fabricated DNA-protected CuNCs and b-CDs for ratiometric determination of acetaminophen and arginine [22]. Metal nanoclusters e.g. CuNCs protected with SH groups can undergo aggregation-induced emission enhancement (AIEE) [23]. External stimuli e.g. metal ions, ethanol, and low pH values can trigger AIEE of these nanoclusters [24]. These stimuli hinder the applications of these nanoclusters; therefore, it is crucial to construct suitable AIEE-inducing factors, making these nanoclusters ideal candidate for (bio) sensing.

The accuracy of determining target concentrations in single-signal output methods is highly dependent on the precise measurement of signal intensities, which may be affected by background signals under varying conditions. This can result in inaccurate measurement of target concentrations, leading to unreliable results. To overcome this limitation, multi-signal output strategies can be employed, which involve the simultaneous detection of multiple signals to enhance the accuracy and reliability of the measurement. Multi-signal output strategies can improve the signal-to-noise ratio and reduce interference from background signals, resulting in more accurate and precise measurements of target concentrations. Therefore, the use of multi-signal output strategies in chemo/bio-sensors can enhance their performance and expand their potential applications [25,26].

Carbon dots (CDs) are a type of nanomaterial composed of carbon atoms arranged in a spherical or quasi-spherical shape. They are typically less than 10 nm in size and exhibit unique optical, electronic, and chemical properties. They can be synthesized using various methods, including chemical oxidation, laser ablation, and microwave irradiation [27,28]. The surface functionalization of carbon dots is important for controlling their properties and enhancing their performance in various applications. Carbon dots can be functionalized with various surface groups, such as amine, carboxyl, and hydroxyl groups, to improve their solubility, stability, and biocompatibility [29,30].

Based on these facts, we have developed a reliable ratiometric-based fluorometric nanosystem for sensing of heparin and protamine. It consists of positively charged blue-emitting carbon dots (B-CDs+) that can aggregate negatively charged thiolate-capped copper nanoclusters (CuNCs) through an electrostatic interaction, improving their fluorescence emission via the aggregation-induced emission enhancement (AIEE) effect. The system is stable under neutral conditions, in watery medium, and at room temperature. Addition of positively charged protamine competes with B-CDs+ and adsorbs onto the surface of CuNCs more than B-CDs, preventing the aggregation of GSH-CuNCs, decreasing their fluorescence emission. Meanwhile, the fluorescence emission of B-CDs+ remains unchanged, acting as an internal reference compound. In the presence of heparin, a negatively charged molecule, the fluorescence emission of GSH-CuNCs is restored as a result of forming strong inter-ionic interaction between heparin and protamine, resulting in the aggregation of GSH-CuNCs by the liberated cationic B-CDs.

Heparin (>96 %), protamine (>90 %), polyethyleneimine (>98 %), chondroitin sulfate (>98 %), dopamine (>98 %), glutathione (>97 %), ascorbic acid (>98 %), glucose (>96 %), cysteine (>97 %), adenine (>98 %), guanine (>98 %), citric acid (AR), CuSO4·5H2O, NaOH, and Tris HCl buffer were procured from Sigma Aldrich, Germany. Cal-heparin® injection (5000 I.U. = 10 mg/mL) and protamine sulfate injection (5 mg/mL) were procured from local markets.
Instrumentation and samples preparation according to our previous reports [32–34] were outlined in the supporting information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of blue-emitting cationic carbon dots (B-CDs+)
ce:section.ce:para: Hydrothermal method was used to synthesize B-CDs+ according to our previous report with slight changes [4]. Briefly, citric acid (1.5 g) was dissolved in 40 mL polyethyleneimine (2.5 mg/mL), and ultrasonicated for 15 min. After that, the mixture was autoclaved at 180 °C for 5 h. The resultant dispersion was centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 25 min. Finally, the solution was dialyzed (MWCO = 3 KDa) before freeze-drying.

Glutathione (10 mL, 60 mM) was slowly added to a solution of CuSO4·5H2O (10 mL, 15 mM). After that, the pH was adjusted to around 4 by addition of 2.0 M NaOH until the solution turns pale yellow. Finally, the resultant solution was preserved at 37 °C for 50 min to obtain GSH-CuNCs [31].

The procedure involved mixing 250 µL of GSH-CuNCs at a concentration of 1.75 mg/mL with 300 µL of B-CDs+ at a concentration of 200 mg/mL, along with 1150 µL of Tris HCl at a pH of 7.0. The resulting mixture was let to stand at room temperature for 3 min. Next, 300 µL of protamine different concentrations were added to the B-CDs+@ GSH-CuNCs probe, followed by another 3 min before recording the fluorescence emission spectra at λex. = 365 nm (Scheme 1 ).

The procedure involved mixing 350 µL of 0.228 mg/ml protamine with 250 µL of 1.75 mg/mL GSH-CuNCs and 300 µL of 200 mg/mL B-CDs+, along with 800 µL of Tris HCl at a pH of 7.0. The resulting mixture was maintained at room temperature for 3 min. Next, 300 µL of heparin different amounts were added to the B-CDs+@ GSH-CuNCs/protamine probe, followed by another 3 min before recording the fluorescence emission spectra at λex. = 365 nm (Scheme 1).

Fig. 1 A displays the TEM image of B-CDs+ where it shows spherical and well-dispersed nanoparticles with an average diameter of 2.2 nm ± 0.13 nm. Fig. 2 B exposes the UV/Vis and fluorescence spectra of the as-fabricated B-CDs+ where it exhibits absorption bands at 235 nm and 315 nm, corresponding to C = C (π-π*) and C = O (n-π*), respectively. Moreover, the B-CDs+ shows an emission band at 470 nm after an excitation at 365 nm. Fig. 1C indicates the XRD of B-CDs+ with diffraction peak at 25.3°, corresponding to (002) diffraction angel of carbon [35]. Fig. 1D implies the FT-IR spectrum of B-CDs+ with absorption bands at 3360 cm−1, 2820 cm−1, 1595 cm−1, and 1280 cm−1, which correspond to υ (OH, NH2), υ (CH2), υ (C = O), and υ (C-O), respectively [36,37]. Fig. 1E displays the fluorescence emission of B-CDs+ under various excitation wavelengths. It is shown that the as-synthesized B-CDs+ probe exhibits different emissions under various excitation wavelengths, which may be attributed to different surface functional groups/ sizes of B-CDs+ [38]. Furthermore, the GSH-CuNCs platform was characterized using TEM (Fig. 2A). The nanoparticles show spherical and well-dispersed nanoshperes with an average diameter of 5.4 nm ± 0.22 nm. Fig. 2B displays the UV/Vis and fluorescence spectra of GSH-CuNCs where it shows absorption bands at 265 nm and 420 nm (weak band). It is noteworthy mentioning that the absence of absorption band at 550–610 nm, which corresponds to surface Plasmon resonsnce of large-sized CuNPs (copper nanoparticles), affirming the formation of tiny CuNCs [39,40]. Meanwhile, the as-fabricated GSH-CuNCs displayed fluorescence emission at 590 nm after excitation at 420 nm with a large stokes shift up to 195 nm, making the probe with ultralow background and light scattering interference. Fig. 2C shows the EDX spectrum of GSH-CuNCs where it displays the presence of carbon, nitrogen, oxygen, nitrogen, sulfur, and copper, suggesting successful functionalization of CuNCs with GSH. Fig. 2D implies the FT-IR spectra of GSH-CuNCs before and after functionalization with GSH. The existence of an absorption band at 2460 cm−1 in Fig. 2D (a), which corresponds to υ (S-H) from GSH, proves the successful functionalization of GSH on the surface of CuNCs. Fig. 2E shows the excitation-dependent emission of GSH-CuNCs where it was found that the emission wavelengths of GSH-CuNCs do not depend on the excitation wavelengths. Fig. S1A shows the XPS survey of GSH-CuNCs where it displays identifiable peaks of S 2p, C 1 s, N 1 s, O 1 s, and Cu 2p. Fig. S1B indicates the high resolution XPS spectra of S 2p with two dominant peaks at 162.2 eV and 163.2 eV, assigning to the sulfur binding of GSH with CuNCs [41]. Fig. S1C displays two important peaks at 932.5 eV and 954.4 eV, which are assigned to the Cu 2p3/2 and Cu 2p1/2, respectively, featuring the existence of Cu°. The lack of satellite peaks at 943 eV proves the absence of Cu2+ in GSH-CuNCs. It is noteworthy mentioning that the difference in the binding energy between Cu° and Cu+ is around 0.1 eV. Therefore, the valence states of Cu in GSH-CuNCs lie between zero and + 1, accordingly to the creation of fluorescent CuNCs where Cu+ on the surface of GSH-CuNCs participated in the fluorescence and stability of the hybrid [42].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The AIEE of GSH-CuNCs by B-CDs+
ce:section.ce:para: Many literatures reported that the AIEE of metal NCs was induced by metal cations and solvents [24], which limit the applications of these NCs in some field such as imaging and biliogical fluids. Therefore, positively chagred blue-emitted carbon dots (B-CDs+) were fabricated which induced the AIEE of GSH-CuNCs via an electrostatic interaction. On the same time, B-CDs+ maintain their blue emission, providing a reference output for a ratiometric measurement. The TEM image of B-CDs+@GSH-CuNCs was shown in Fig. S2A, where it was found that the particle size of B-CDs+@GSH-CuNCs (0.3 µm) is increased significantly upon mixing. Furthermore, dynamic light scattering (DLS) was used to describe the hydrodynamic diameter of B-CDs+, GSH-CuNCs, and B-CDs+@GSH-CuNCs (Fig. S2B). I t was found that the hydrodyanic diameter of B-CDs+ is 43.6 nm, while that of GSH-CuNCs is 55.4 nm. It is noteworthy to mention that the particle sizes obtained by DLS are higher than that estimated by TEM, which may be ascribed to the extent of hydration and swelling of these nanoparticles in water [24]. The average particle size of B-CDs+@GSH-CuNCs is estimated to be 325 nm, which is more than that of B-CDs+ and GSH-CuNCs, confirming the self-assembly of GSH-CuNCs triggered by the addition of B-CDs+. To confirm that the interaction between B-CDs+ and GSH-CuNCs is an electrostatic interaction, Zeta-potentials of B-CDs+, GSH-CuNCs, and B-CDs+@GSH-CuNCs were measured (Fig. S2C). It was found that the Zeta-potential of B-CDs+ in Tris HCl buffer of pH 7.0 is + 23.8 mV as a result of exceesive amount of NH2 moiety of polyethyleneimine. The Zeta-potential of GSH-CuNCs is −21.3 mV as a result of negatively charged GSH on the surface of CuNCs (isoelectric point is 2.87) while the Zeta potential of B-CDs+@GSH-CuNCs is 3.8 mV. The data obtained from Zeta-potential measurements proves that the electrostatic interaction is a possible force between B-CDs+ and GSH-CuNCs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric perception of protamine and heparin and variables optimization
ce:section.ce:para: The as-fabricated nanosystem exhibited two emission bands at 470 nm and 590 nm due to B-CDs+ and GSH-CuNCs, respectively (Fig. S3). It is clearly seen that heparin does not affect the emission intensity of GSH-CuNCs, while the addition of protamine decreases the fluorescence emission of GSH-CuNCs at 590 nm, which may be ascribed to the inhibition of GSH-CuNCs self-assembly. Addition of heparin to CDs+@GSH-CuNCs/protamine system recovered the fluorescence emission of GSH-CuNCs as a result of strong electrostatic interaction between positively charged protamine and negatively charged heparin, favoring the self-assembly of GSH-CuNCs by B-CDs+. Fig. S4A and Fig. S4B display the TEM images before and after addition of protamine, respectively. It was obvious that the addition of protamine to B-CDs+@GSH-CuNCs produces smaller particles, which suggests that the electrostatic interaction between protamine and GSH-CuNCs is much stronger than that between GSH-CuNCs and B-CDs+. These data agreed well with that obtained with DLS data as depicted in Fig. S4C. After addition of protamine, the particle size diameter of B-CDs+@GSH-CuNCs is reduced from 325 nm to 103 nm, suggesting that protamine disassembles B-CDs+@GSH-CuNCs. Fig.S5 exhibits the fluorescence spectra of B-CDs+ and GSH-CuNCs in the presence of protamine and heparin. It was concluded that the emission intensities of B-CDs+ and GSH-CuNCs were not significantly influenced by the addition of protamine and heparin. These data prove that protamine can quench the fluorescence emission produced from the AIEE which is induced by B-CDs+ rather than the own emission of GSH-AuNCs.

In order to improve the detection sensitivity towards protamine and heparin, variables affecting the reaction with D-CDs+ and GSH-CuNCs including incubation temperature, pH, and reaction time (Fig.S6). Fig.S6A shows the influence of incubation temperature on the response of D-CDs+ and GSH-CuNCs towards protamine where the fluorescence ratio (F590/F470) was decreased with increasing the temperature. Therefore, room temperature was selected as an ideal value for reaction between D-CDs+ and GSH-CuNCs and protamine. Moreover, the influence of pH and reaction time were investigated in Fig.S6B and Fig.S6C where it was found the optimum values for reaction pH and time are 7.0 and 3 min, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric quantitative estimation of protamine and heparin
ce:section.ce:para: The quantitative detection of protamine using B-CDs+@GSH-CuNCs nanosystem is achieved under optimal conditions. As depicts in Fig. 3 A, the fluorescence emission of GSH-CuNCs 590 nm gradually decreases with an increase in protamine concentration, while the emission of B-CDs+ at 470 nm remains almost constant. The fluorescence response (F470/F590) is directly proportional to the protamine concentration within the range of 0.004 to 40 μg/mL (as shown in Fig. 3B). The LOD is determined to be 0.001 μg/mL (S/N = 3).

Exposure to heparin causes the self-assembly of GSH-CuNCs and B-CDs+ to resume by efficiently interacting with protamine and releasing it from the surface of GSH-CuNCs, resulting in the recovery of fluorescence at 590 nm. As depicted in Fig. 3C, the fluorescence intensity of GSH-CuNCs gradually increases with an increase in heparin concentration, while that of B-CDs+ remains constant. The linear response to heparin ranges from 0.01 to 28 μg/mL, with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.004 μg/mL (S/N = 3) (as shown in Fig. 3D). The B-CDs+@GSH-CuNCs hybrid probe utilized in this assay offers a unique advantage over other methods by combining the signal amplification ability of the AIEE feature with the dual-emission probe's self-calibration function. Table S1 outlines the analytical performance of the B-CDs+@GSH-CuNCs nanosystem and other reported systems for measuring protamine and heparin. Results indicate that the proposed system has a broad linear dynamic range, low detection limit, high detection reliability, and fast response time.

In order to evaluate the selectivity of the B-CDs+@GSH-CuNCs nanosystem, the effect of coexisting species were evaluated including Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, NH4 +, Cl-, HCO3 –, CO3 2–, SO4 2-, NO3 –, chondroitin sulfate, dopamine, glutathione, ascorbic acid, glucose, cysteine, adenine, guanine, uric acid, urea, and human serum albumin. It is clearly seen that the coexisting interfering species did not significantly affect the fluorescence ratio (F590/470) (Fig. 4 A). However, protamine produces prominent change in the fluorescence response, suggesting the selectivity of B-CDs+@GSH-CuNCs nanosystem to protamine sensing. Protamine is a protein with a high positive charge, consisting of numerous arginine residues. It possesses approximately 7–8 positive charges per KDa of protein, which is notably higher compared to typical biological molecules. As a result, protamine exhibits a more robust electrostatic interaction with the negatively charged probe [43]. In addition, the selectivity of B-CDs+@GSH-CuNCs/protamine nanosystem towards heparin was investigated in Fig. 4B. It was concluded that the nanosystem exhibits good selectivity towards heparin in the presence of coexisting species. The high selectivity of B-CDs+@GSH-CuNCs/protamine system towards heparin detection may be attributed to the specific electrostatic interaction between them to form a stable ion pair complex [44].

The B-CDs+@GSH-CuNCs and B-CDs+@GSH-CuNCs/protamine nanosystems were used to evaluate the feasibility of the proposed strategy for the determination of protamine and heparin in human serum and urine samples. Tables 1 and 2
states the recoveries % of protamine and heparin are in the range of 96.8–105.3 % and 96.0–103.7 % with RSDs value in the range of 2.4 %-3.6 % and 2.1 %-3.5 %, respectively, affirming the accuracy of the fabricated nanosystems. Moreover, the proposed systems were compared to other reported methods for determination of protamine and heparin. The study found that the proposed strategy yielded results that were not significantly different from those obtained using the previously reported methods, thereby demonstrating the accuracy of the proposed approach.

The present study introduces a new nanosystem for the ratiometric determination of protamine and heparin. This nanosystem is composed of negatively charged GSH-capped CuNCs (GSH-CuNCs) and positively charged polyethyleneimine-modified carbon dots (B-CDs+). The self-assembly of GSH-CuNCs with B-CDs+ promotes the fluorescence emission via AIEE. In the existence of protamine, a polycationic protein, the GSH-CuNCs fluorescence at 590 nm is significantly reduced. Meanwhile, the fluorescence emission of B-CDs+ remains constant, facilitating the determination of protamine. Upon the addition of heparin to a solution containing B-CDs+@GSH-CuNCs/protamine, the fluorescence emission of the nanosystem is restored due to the strong electrostatic interaction between protamine and heparin, leading to the self-assembly of the GSH-CuNCs probe by CDs+. The proposed fluorometric system offers several advantages, including an ultra-low LODs, wide dynamic linear range, good anti-interference, satisfactory detection reliability, and short response time. Importantly, this strategy was devoted to determine protamine and heparin in serum and urine samples, and the results are acceptable.

Saad A. Alkahtani: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Resources, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Ashraf M. Mahmoud: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Resources, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Rasha M.K. Mohamed: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Resources, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Mohamed M. El-Wekil: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Resources, Validation, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are thankful to the Deanship of Scientific Research at Najran University for funding this work under the National Research Priorities funding program grant code (NU/DRP/MRC/12/22).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2023.109404.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A reliable ratiometric fluorescence sensing of heparin and its antidote based on cationic carbon quantum dots and acid red 87
Microchem. J.
